questiontext.text: Two continuous maps \(f, g: X \to Y\) are homotopic (\(f \simeq g\)) if:

answer.text: There exists a continuous map \(H: X \times [0,1] \to Y\) with \(H(x,0) = f(x)\) and \(H(x,1) = g(x)\) for all \(x \in X\).

feedback.text: Correct. A homotopy H is a continuous deformation of f into g parameterized by t∈[0,1]. Homotopy is an equivalence relation on continuous maps, and homotopy classes are the fundamental objects in algebraic topology.

answer.text: There exists a homeomorphism \(h: X \to Y\) with \(h \circ f = g\).

feedback.text: This describes a different (stronger) relation. Homotopy does not require a homeomorphism; it requires a continuous one-parameter family connecting f and g.

answer.text: \(f\) and \(g\) are equal as functions: \(f(x) = g(x)\) for all \(x\).

feedback.text: Equal functions are homotopic (the constant homotopy H(x,t)=f(x) works), but homotopic functions need not be equal. Homotopy is much weaker than equality.
Having homeomorphic images is unrelated to homotopy. Two maps can be homotopic even if their images are very different subsets of Y.

questiontext.text: The fundamental group of the circle \(S^1\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. π₁(S¹) ≅ ℤ, with generator given by the loop going around S¹ once counterclockwise. The integer n corresponds to the loop winding n times around the circle. This is computed using the universal cover ℝ → S¹ via t↦e^{2πit}.
ℤ/2ℤ is the fundamental group of ℝP¹ (the real projective line, which is homeomorphic to S¹ as a space, but... wait ℝP¹ ≅ S¹, so π₁(ℝP¹)=ℤ as well). π₁(S¹)=ℤ, not ℤ/2ℤ.
The trivial fundamental group belongs to simply connected spaces (like S², ℝⁿ, contractible spaces). S¹ is not simply connected: loops that wind around the circle cannot be contracted to a point.
π₁(S¹) ≅ ℤ (integers, not rationals). The winding number of a loop is always an integer.

questiontext.text: By the Seifert-van Kampen theorem, the fundamental group of the torus \(T^2 = S^1 \times S^1\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. Since T²=S¹×S¹ and π₁ of a product is the product of fundamental groups (for path-connected spaces), π₁(T²) = π₁(S¹)×π₁(S¹) = ℤ×ℤ. The two generators a,b commute: aba⁻¹b⁻¹=1 in π₁(T²).
F₂ (free group on 2 generators) is the fundamental group of the figure-eight (wedge of two circles), not the torus. In F₂, the generators do not commute: ab≠ba. In π₁(T²)=ℤ×ℤ, they do commute.
ℤ/2ℤ×ℤ/2ℤ is finite. π₁(T²)=ℤ×ℤ is infinite. This is the fundamental group of the Klein bottle or other surfaces, not the torus.
The torus is not simply connected. Its fundamental group ℤ×ℤ has two independent non-contractible loops (going around each circle factor).

questiontext.text: Which of the following spaces is simply connected (connected with trivial fundamental group)?

feedback.text: Correct. π₁(S²)={e} (trivial). Every loop on S² can be contracted to a point (S² is simply connected). More generally, Sⁿ is simply connected for n≥2. The universal cover of S² is S² itself.
π₁(S¹) = ℤ ≠ {e}. S¹ is not simply connected: a loop that winds around the circle cannot be contracted.
π₁(T²) = ℤ×ℤ ≠ {e}. The torus is not simply connected.
π₁(ℝ²⧵{0}) = ℤ (homotopy equivalent to S¹). The origin creates a "hole" around which loops can wind, so the space is not simply connected.

questiontext.text: The universal cover of \(S^1\) is:

answer.text: \(\mathbb{R}\), with covering map \(p: \mathbb{R} \to S^1\) given by \(p(t) = e^{2\pi i t}\).

feedback.text: Correct. ℝ is simply connected and p:ℝ→S¹ defined by p(t)=e^{2πit} is a covering map (each point of S¹ has evenly covered neighborhoods). The deck transformations form the group ℤ (shift by integers), consistent with π₁(S¹)=ℤ.

answer.text: \(S^1\) itself (the identity map is the covering).

feedback.text: The identity cover is trivial (degree 1). The UNIVERSAL cover is the simply connected cover, which for S¹ is ℝ (not S¹ itself, since π₁(S¹)=ℤ≠{e}).
T² has fundamental group ℤ×ℤ and is not simply connected. The universal cover of S¹ must be simply connected; ℝ (not T²) plays this role.
Stereographic projection is a homeomorphism between S²⧵{N} and ℝ², not a covering of S¹. The universal cover of S¹ is ℝ.

questiontext.text: Two spaces \(X\) and \(Y\) are homotopy equivalent (\(X \simeq Y\)) if:

answer.text: There exist continuous maps \(f: X \to Y\) and \(g: Y \to X\) such that \(g \circ f \simeq \mathrm{id}_X\) and \(f \circ g \simeq \mathrm{id}_Y\).

feedback.text: Correct. Homotopy equivalence is the "topological isomorphism" of algebraic topology: it preserves all homotopy groups, homology, cohomology, etc. Homeomorphism ⟹ homotopy equivalence but not conversely (e.g., ℝⁿ ≃ {pt} via contraction).

answer.text: There is a homeomorphism \(f: X \to Y\).

feedback.text: Homeomorphism implies homotopy equivalence but is strictly stronger. Homotopy equivalence allows the spaces to be "deformed" into each other, not necessarily bijectively mapped.
Same cardinality is much weaker and unrelated to homotopy equivalence. [0,1] and ℝ² have the same cardinality (uncountable) but are not homotopy equivalent.

answer.text: Every map \(f: X \to Y\) is null-homotopic (homotopic to a constant map).

feedback.text: This condition would mean Y is "homotopically trivial" from X's perspective, not that X and Y are homotopy equivalent.

questiontext.text: A space \(X\) is contractible if and only if:

answer.text: The identity map \(\mathrm{id}_X\) is null-homotopic (homotopic to a constant map), equivalently \(X \simeq \{pt\}\).

feedback.text: Correct. Contractible means homotopy equivalent to a point. Examples: ℝⁿ (contract radially to origin), convex subsets, star-shaped sets. Contractible ⟹ all homotopy groups trivial ⟹ all homology groups trivial (acyclic). The converse does not hold in general.

answer.text: Every loop in \(X\) can be contracted to a point (simply connected).

feedback.text: Simply connected means π₁(X)={e} (every loop contracts). Contractible is stronger: ALL higher homotopy groups also vanish (πₙ(X)={e} for all n). Contractible ⟹ simply connected but not conversely (S² is simply connected but not contractible).
A single point is trivially contractible, but contractible spaces can have many points (e.g., ℝⁿ). Contractible means homotopy equivalent to {pt}, not homeomorphic to {pt}.
Compact + simply connected does not imply contractible. S² is compact and simply connected but NOT contractible (π₂(S²)=ℤ≠{e}).

questiontext.text: The second homotopy group \(\pi_2(S^2)\) is:

answer.text: \(\mathbb{Z}\), generated by the identity map \(\mathrm{id}: S^2 \to S^2\).

feedback.text: Correct. π₂(S²) = ℤ (Hopf, 1931). The generator is the identity map S²→S², corresponding to the "degree 1" map. The Hopf fibration S³→S² has the Hopf map as a non-trivial element of π₃(S²)=ℤ.
Simple connectedness means π₁=0 but says nothing about higher homotopy groups. π₂(S²)=ℤ≠{e}. The sphere S² has a rich homotopy structure.
π₂(S²)=ℤ (infinite cyclic). ℤ/2ℤ appears in other homotopy groups of spheres (like π₃(S²)=ℤ is also interesting via Hopf).
π₂(S²)=ℤ (rank 1, not 2). The Hurewicz theorem gives π₂(S²)≅H₂(S²)=ℤ since S² is simply connected.

questiontext.text: The fundamental group of the figure-eight space \(S^1 \vee S^1\) (wedge of two circles) is:

answer.text: The free group \(F_2 = \langle a, b \rangle\) on two generators (with no relations).

feedback.text: Correct. By Seifert-van Kampen: S¹∨S¹ = U∪V where U≃S¹, V≃S¹, U∩V≃{pt} (contractible). So π₁(S¹∨S¹) = π₁(S¹) * π₁(S¹) = ℤ*ℤ = F₂ (free product, which for groups of integers is the free group on 2 generators).
ℤ×ℤ is the fundamental group of the TORUS T²=S¹×S¹ (product), not the WEDGE S¹∨S¹. In the torus, the generators commute; in the wedge/figure-eight, they do not.
This is the fundamental group of ℝP²#ℝP² (Klein bottle), not the figure-eight. The figure-eight has generators of infinite order.
The figure-eight has a non-contractible loop around each circle, so its fundamental group is non-trivial (it is the free group F₂).

questiontext.text: The Brouwer fixed-point theorem states: every continuous map \(f: D^n \to D^n\) (closed \(n\)-disk) has a fixed point. A topological proof uses:

answer.text: The fact that there is no retraction \(r: D^n \to S^{n-1}\) (the disk does not retract onto its boundary), which follows from the non-triviality of \(H_{n-1}(S^{n-1}) \cong \mathbb{Z}\).

feedback.text: Correct. If f had no fixed point, we could define a retraction D^n→S^{n-1} (shoot a ray from f(x) through x to the boundary), contradicting the fact that such a retraction cannot exist (S^{n-1} has non-trivial (n−1)-st homology while D^n is contractible).

answer.text: The intermediate value theorem (applicable only in dimension \(n=1\)).

feedback.text: The IVT proof works for n=1 (f:[−1,1]→[−1,1] must have a fixed point by IVT). But the general Brouwer theorem requires homology/homotopy theory, not just IVT.

answer.text: The Baire category theorem applied to the space of maps \(D^n \to D^n\).

feedback.text: Baire category theorem is not used in the proof of Brouwer's fixed-point theorem. The proof uses algebraic topology (homology or degree theory).

answer.text: Compactness of \(D^n\): every sequence has a convergent subsequence.

feedback.text: Compactness is needed for Schauder's fixed-point theorem (infinite-dimensional version), but Brouwer's theorem for D^n specifically uses the topology of D^n via algebraic invariants, not just compactness.

questiontext.text: For path-connected spaces \(X\) and \(Y\), \(\pi_1(X \times Y)\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. For path-connected spaces, the fundamental group of a product is the direct product: π₁(X×Y) ≅ π₁(X)×π₁(Y). This follows because loops in X×Y project to loops in X and Y, and the two factors commute in π₁(X×Y).
Free product arises from wedge (X∨Y), not product (X×Y). For the product, the fundamental group is the direct product.

answer.text: \(\pi_1(X) \oplus \pi_1(Y)\) (direct sum, distinct from direct product for infinite groups).

feedback.text: For groups, direct product and direct sum coincide for finitely many factors. So π₁(X×Y)=π₁(X)×π₁(Y)=π₁(X)⊕π₁(Y) (same group). This is correct but equivalent to the previous answer.

answer.text: \(\pi_1(X \times Y) = \{e\}\) if either \(\pi_1(X)\) or \(\pi_1(Y)\) is trivial.

feedback.text: π₁(X×Y)=π₁(X)×π₁(Y). If π₁(X)={e} then π₁(X×Y)=π₁(Y), not {e} (unless π₁(Y)={e} too). The product formula holds regardless.

questiontext.text: A CW-complex is constructed by:

answer.text: Attaching \(n\)-cells \(e^n \cong D^n\) to a previously constructed \((n-1)\)-skeleton via attaching maps \(\phi: S^{n-1} \to X^{n-1}\).

feedback.text: Correct. CW-complexes (Closure-finite Weak topology) are built inductively: start with 0-cells (points), attach 1-cells (edges), 2-cells (disks), etc. Most spaces of interest in algebraic topology (manifolds, spheres, projective spaces) are CW-complexes.

answer.text: Taking the metric completion of a simplicial complex.

feedback.text: Simplicial complexes are related to CW-complexes (every simplicial complex is a CW-complex) but metric completion is a different operation. CW-complexes are defined by cell-attaching, not metric completion.

answer.text: Taking a quotient of a manifold by a group action.

feedback.text: Quotient by group actions gives orbit spaces, which can often be given CW-structures, but this is not the definition of a CW-complex.

answer.text: Using only 0-cells and 1-cells (graphs are the only CW-complexes).

feedback.text: CW-complexes include cells of all dimensions. Graphs are 1-dimensional CW-complexes; CW-complexes can be of any dimension (including infinite).

questiontext.text: A retraction of \(X\) onto a subspace \(A \subseteq X\) is a continuous map \(r: X \to A\) such that \(r|_A = \mathrm{id}_A\). Which statement is correct?

answer.text: If \(r: X \to A\) is a retraction, the induced map \(r_*: \pi_1(X) \to \pi_1(A)\) is surjective, and \(\pi_1(A)\) is a retract of \(\pi_1(X)\).

feedback.text: Correct. The retraction r gives a right inverse for the inclusion i:A↪X at the level of fundamental groups: r∘i = id_A implies r_*∘i_* = id, so r_* is surjective and π₁(A) is a retract (quotient) of π₁(X). This is used to prove non-retractability (like S¹ ↪ D²).

answer.text: A retraction always exists between any two spaces.

feedback.text: Retractions do not always exist. For example, there is no retraction D²→S¹ (this is equivalent to Brouwer's fixed-point theorem). The existence of a retraction imposes strong topological constraints.

answer.text: A retraction \(r: X \to A\) is a homeomorphism.

feedback.text: A retraction is generally not a homeomorphism (it collapses the complement X⧵A onto A). A retraction is a surjection but not injective unless A=X.

answer.text: If a retraction \(r: X \to A\) exists, then \(X\) and \(A\) are homeomorphic.

feedback.text: Retracts need not be homeomorphic to the whole space. For example, any point is a retract of a connected space via the constant map, but a point is not homeomorphic to the space.

questiontext.text: For a universal covering space \(p: \tilde{X} \to X\), the group of deck transformations (covering transformations) is isomorphic to:

answer.text: \(\pi_1(X, x_0)\) (the fundamental group of the base space).

feedback.text: Correct. For a universal cover, the deck transformation group Aut(p) ≅ π₁(X). For example, the deck transformations of ℝ→S¹ are integer shifts t↦t+n, forming a group isomorphic to ℤ≅π₁(S¹).
The universal cover is simply connected: π₁(X̃)={e}. The deck transformations are isomorphic to π₁(X) (the base), not π₁(X̃).
Deck transformations are homeomorphisms of X̃ preserving the covering structure (i.e., p∘φ=p). This is a proper subgroup of all homeomorphisms of X̃.

answer.text: A trivial group (deck transformations are always the identity).

feedback.text: Deck transformations are trivial only when X is simply connected. For S¹ with universal cover ℝ, the deck transformations form the infinite group ℤ≅π₁(S¹).

questiontext.text: Suppose \(X = U \cup V\) where \(U, V\) are open and path-connected, and \(U \cap V\) is path-connected. By the Seifert-van Kampen theorem, \(\pi_1(X)\) is the pushout:

feedback.text: Correct. The Seifert-van Kampen theorem gives π₁(X) as the amalgamated free product (pushout in the category of groups): generators from U and V with relations from U∩V. When U∩V is contractible, this is the free product π₁(U)*π₁(V).
Direct products arise from products of spaces (X=U×V), not unions. Van Kampen gives an amalgamated free product (pushout), which is a direct product only in special cases.
Only the intersection's fundamental group is not enough. Van Kampen combines π₁(U) and π₁(V) with relations from π₁(U∩V) via the amalgamated free product.

answer.text: \(\pi_1(X) = \{e\}\) if both \(U\) and \(V\) are simply connected.

feedback.text: If π₁(U)=π₁(V)={e}, then π₁(U)*_{π₁(U∩V)}π₁(V) = {e}*_{π₁(U∩V)}{e} = π₁(U∩V)/N where N is the normal closure of the image of π₁(U∩V)... Actually if π₁(U)=π₁(V)={e}, then the amalgamated product is trivial {e}, so X is simply connected. This option is actually correct in this case, but it is a consequence, not the general statement of Van Kampen.

questiontext.text: A space \(A \subseteq X\) is a deformation retract of \(X\) if there is a continuous \(H: X \times [0,1] \to X\) with \(H(x,0) = x\), \(H(x,1) \in A\) for all \(x\), and \(H(a,t) = a\) for all \(a \in A, t \in [0,1]\). What does this imply?

feedback.text: Correct. A deformation retract is a special homotopy equivalence: the inclusion i:A↪X is a homotopy equivalence with homotopy inverse r=H(−,1). Since homotopy equivalent spaces have isomorphic fundamental groups (and all homotopy groups), π₁(A)≅π₁(X).
Deformation retract implies homotopy equivalence, not homeomorphism. For example, ℝⁿ deformation retracts to {0} (contractible), but ℝⁿ and {0} are not homeomorphic.

answer.text: X is compact if and only if A is compact.

feedback.text: Deformation retracts preserve homotopy type, not compactness. For example, D² deformation retracts to {0} (compact to compact here), but in general the relationship between X and A's compactness can vary.
Deformation retracts can involve non-closed subsets in general, though in nice spaces (like ANRs) they are often closed. Closedness is not required by the definition.

questiontext.text: The Klein bottle \(K\) can be described as a quotient of the square \([0,1]^2\) with edge identifications \(aba^{-1}b\) (identifying opposite sides, one with a twist). Its fundamental group is:

feedback.text: Correct. The Klein bottle has fundamental group with presentation ⟨a,b | aba⁻¹=b⁻¹⟩ or equivalently ⟨a,b | abab⁻¹=1⟩. This group is not abelian (unlike the torus, where abab⁻¹=aba⁻¹b⁻¹·ab∼... the torus has [a,b]=1). The Klein bottle is non-orientable.
The torus has π₁=ℤ×ℤ with relation [a,b]=aba⁻¹b⁻¹=1. The Klein bottle has a different relation (aba⁻¹=b⁻¹), giving a non-abelian fundamental group. The Klein bottle is NOT homeomorphic to the torus.
π₁(K) is a non-abelian group with two generators and one relation, not isomorphic to ℤ (which is cyclic and abelian).
The Klein bottle has non-trivial fundamental group ⟨a,b|abab⁻¹=1⟩. It is not simply connected.

questiontext.text: The Euler characteristic \(\chi(S)\) of a compact surface relates to its genus \(g\) by:

answer.text: \(\chi = 2 - 2g\) for orientable surfaces (genus-\(g\) surface \(\Sigma_g\)); \(\chi = 2 - k\) for non-orientable surfaces (with \(k\) crosscaps).

feedback.text: Correct. For the sphere: g=0, χ=2. Torus: g=1, χ=0. Double torus: g=2, χ=−2. Klein bottle: k=2, χ=0. ℝP²: k=1, χ=1. The Euler characteristic is a topological invariant computed from any triangulation: χ=V−E+F.
Incorrect. χ=2−2g for orientable surfaces, so χ=g only when 2−2g=g, i.e., g=2/3, which is not an integer. The correct formula is χ=2−2g.
Wrong sign: χ=2−2g, not 2g−2. For g=0 (sphere): χ=2 (correct), but 2(0)−2=−2 (wrong).

answer.text: \(\chi\) depends on the triangulation chosen, not just the surface.

feedback.text: By the invariance of the Euler characteristic, χ = V−E+F is the same for all triangulations of a given surface. It is a topological invariant.

questiontext.text: The real projective plane \(\mathbb{RP}^2\) has fundamental group:

feedback.text: Correct. ℝP² = S²/{±1} (antipodal identification). The universal cover is S² (simply connected), with deck transformations {+1,−1}≅ℤ/2ℤ. So π₁(ℝP²)=ℤ/2ℤ. The non-trivial element corresponds to a path from a point to its antipode (a non-contractible loop in ℝP²).
ℤ is the fundamental group of S¹ or ℝP¹. For ℝP²=S²/Z₂, the fundamental group is ℤ/2ℤ (order 2), not ℤ.
ℝP² is NOT simply connected: π₁(ℝP²)=ℤ/2ℤ≠{e}. Only ℝPⁿ for n=1 has fundamental group ℤ; for n≥2 it is ℤ/2ℤ.
This is too large. π₁(ℝP²)=ℤ/2ℤ (order 2), not ℤ×ℤ/2ℤ (infinite group).

questiontext.text: The Lefschetz fixed-point theorem states: if \(f: X \to X\) is a continuous map on a compact triangulable space and the Lefschetz number \(\Lambda(f) = \sum_k (-1)^k \mathrm{tr}(f_*: H_k \to H_k) \neq 0\), then:

feedback.text: Correct. The Lefschetz fixed-point theorem: Λ(f)≠0 implies f has a fixed point. For f=identity: Λ(id)=χ(X) (Euler characteristic). Brouwer's theorem is a special case: on D^n, Λ(f)=1≠0 for any f, guaranteeing a fixed point.
Λ(f)≠0 implies the EXISTENCE of fixed points, not their absence. Λ(f)=0 is necessary (but not sufficient) for f to have no fixed points.
The Lefschetz number concerns fixed points, not null-homotopy. A null-homotopic map has Λ(f)=Λ(const)=1 (for connected X), which implies a fixed point. But Λ(f)≠0 does not imply null-homotopy.
The theorem applies to any compact triangulable space, not just contractible ones. S² is not contractible but the Lefschetz theorem applies to maps on S².
